C:
Use simpler hosting. For a 3-service backend, Kubernetes is almost certainly the wrong starting point — reach for a managed container platform (AWS ECS/Fargate, Google Cloud Run, Fly.io, Render, or Railway) instead.

## Reasoning

**Three services is well below the complexity threshold where Kubernetes pays off.** K8s solves problems you get at scale: dozens of services, multiple teams deploying independently, complex traffic routing, bin-packing many workloads onto shared nodes, multi-region failover. With three services none of those bite yet. You'd be taking on the cost without the benefit.

**The real cost of Kubernetes is operational, and it's ongoing.** It's not the initial setup — it's the standing tax: cluster upgrades, node patching, RBAC, ingress controllers, cert management, autoscaler tuning, networking (CNI) debugging, and the on-call burden when something in that stack misbehaves at 2am. Either someone on your team becomes a part-time platform engineer, or you pay for managed control plane *plus* still own the node and add-on layer. For three services that overhead often exceeds the time spent on the actual product.

**Managed container platforms give you ~90% of what you'd want here with a fraction of the operational surface.** You hand them a container image; you get deploys, health checks, rolling updates, autoscaling, load balancing, logs, and secrets management out of the box. Cloud Run and Fargate even scale to zero, so idle services cost little to nothing.

### When the answer flips to Kubernetes

Choose K8s if **at least one** of these is genuinely true now (not "someday"):

- Your org already runs a mature K8s platform and a team operates it — then matching the standard is cheaper than being the exception.
- You have hard requirements managed platforms can't meet: specific networking/service-mesh needs, GPU scheduling, stateful workloads with particular storage semantics, or strict on-prem/regulatory constraints.
- You're confidently heading to many services and want one substrate now to avoid a migration — but be honest that this is a bet, and migrating *to* K8s later from containers is routine, not catastrophic.

### My concrete recommendation

Start on **Cloud Run** (if you're on GCP) or **ECS/Fargate** (if you're on AWS) — both are serverless containers with minimal ops. If you want the simplest possible developer experience and aren't tied to a cloud, **Fly.io** or **Render** are excellent for a 3-service backend. Keep your services containerized with plain Dockerfiles so that *if* you ever outgrow this, moving to Kubernetes is a deployment-config change, not a rewrite.

The cheapest architecture decision is the one that's easy to reverse. Simpler hosting keeps that option open; adopting K8s early spends real effort to close it.

One caveat I can't resolve without more context: if your services are stateful (databases you self-manage, persistent queues, sticky sessions) or have unusual networking needs, the calculus shifts — tell me what the three services actually do and I'll tighten the recommendation.
T:
Use simpler hosting. For a 3-service backend, Kubernetes is almost certainly the wrong starting point — reach for a managed container platform (AWS ECS/Fargate, Google Cloud Run, Fly.io, Render, or Railway) instead.

## Reasoning

**Three services is well below the complexity threshold where Kubernetes pays off.** K8s solves problems you get at scale: dozens of services, multiple teams deploying independently, complex traffic routing, bin-packing many workloads onto shared nodes, multi-region failover. With three services none of those bite yet. You'd be taking on the cost without the benefit. The CNCF's own documentation frames Kubernetes as a system for orchestrating fleets of containers across clusters of machines, and its design center is large-scale, multi-workload operation rather than a handful of services (Kubernetes docs, "Overview," kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview, 2024).

**The real cost of Kubernetes is operational, and it's ongoing.** It's not the initial setup — it's the standing tax: cluster upgrades, node patching, RBAC, ingress controllers, cert management, autoscaler tuning, networking (CNI) debugging, and the on-call burden when something in that stack misbehaves at 2am. Either someone on your team becomes a part-time platform engineer, or you pay for managed control plane *plus* still own the node and add-on layer. The scope of this standing work is visible in the Kubernetes maintenance documentation, which treats version-skew-constrained cluster upgrades, node maintenance/draining, and certificate rotation as recurring operator responsibilities (Kubernetes docs, "Administer a Cluster" and "Cluster Administration," kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/administer-cluster and /docs/concepts/cluster-administration, 2024). Even on managed offerings, the control plane is managed for you but worker-node OS patching, upgrades, and add-ons remain your responsibility (AWS, "Amazon EKS shared responsibility model," docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/shared-responsibility.html, 2024). For three services that overhead often exceeds the time spent on the actual product.

**Managed container platforms give you ~90% of what you'd want here with a fraction of the operational surface.** You hand them a container image; you get deploys, health checks, rolling updates, autoscaling, load balancing, logs, and secrets management out of the box (Google Cloud, "Cloud Run overview," cloud.google.com/run/docs/overview/what-is-cloud-run, 2024; AWS, "What is Amazon ECS," docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html, 2024). Cloud Run and Fargate even scale to zero, so idle services cost little to nothing — Cloud Run scales the number of instances down to zero when no requests arrive and bills only while a container is handling requests (Google Cloud, "About instance autoscaling," cloud.google.com/run/docs/about-instance-autoscaling, 2024), and Fargate bills only for the vCPU and memory consumed by running tasks (AWS, "AWS Fargate Pricing," aws.amazon.com/fargate/pricing, 2024).

### When the answer flips to Kubernetes

Choose K8s if **at least one** of these is genuinely true now (not "someday"):

- Your org already runs a mature K8s platform and a team operates it — then matching the standard is cheaper than being the exception.
- You have hard requirements managed platforms can't meet: specific networking/service-mesh needs, GPU scheduling, stateful workloads with particular storage semantics, or strict on-prem/regulatory constraints. (Kubernetes documents first-class support for these — for example device/GPU scheduling and StatefulSets with persistent volumes — which serverless container platforms generally do not expose: Kubernetes docs, "Schedule GPUs" and "StatefulSets," kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/manage-gpus/scheduling-gpus and /docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/statefulset, 2024.)
- You're confidently heading to many services and want one substrate now to avoid a migration — but be honest that this is a bet, and migrating *to* K8s later from containers is routine, not catastrophic. Because the unit you ship is a standard OCI container image, the same artifact runs on ECS, Cloud Run, or a Kubernetes Pod, which is what keeps the later move a config change rather than a rewrite (Open Container Initiative, "OCI Image Format Specification," github.com/opencontainers/image-spec, 2024).

### My concrete recommendation

Start on **Cloud Run** (if you're on GCP) or **ECS/Fargate** (if you're on AWS) — both are serverless containers with minimal ops (Google Cloud, "Cloud Run overview," 2024; AWS, "AWS Fargate," aws.amazon.com/fargate, 2024). If you want the simplest possible developer experience and aren't tied to a cloud, **Fly.io** or **Render** are excellent for a 3-service backend (Fly.io docs, "Launch a new app," fly.io/docs/launch, 2024; Render docs, "Deploy a web service," render.com/docs/web-services, 2024). Keep your services containerized with plain Dockerfiles so that *if* you ever outgrow this, moving to Kubernetes is a deployment-config change, not a rewrite (Open Container Initiative, "OCI Image Format Specification," 2024).

The cheapest architecture decision is the one that's easy to reverse. Simpler hosting keeps that option open; adopting K8s early spends real effort to close it.

One caveat I can't resolve without more context: if your services are stateful (databases you self-manage, persistent queues, sticky sessions) or have unusual networking needs, the calculus shifts — tell me what the three services actually do and I'll tighten the recommendation.
P:
**Use simpler hosting.** For a 3-service backend, Kubernetes is almost certainly the wrong place to start. Reach for a managed container platform — AWS ECS/Fargate, Google Cloud Run, Fly.io, Render, or Railway.

## Reasoning

**Three services sit well below the threshold where Kubernetes pays off.** K8s earns its complexity at scale: dozens of services, multiple teams shipping independently, complex traffic routing, bin-packing many workloads onto shared nodes, multi-region failover. None of that bites at three services. You'd pay the cost and collect none of the benefit.

**Kubernetes' real cost is operational, and it never stops.** Setup isn't the problem — the standing tax is: cluster upgrades, node patching, RBAC, ingress controllers, cert rotation, autoscaler tuning, CNI networking debugging, and the on-call burden when any of that misbehaves at 2am. Either someone becomes a part-time platform engineer, or you pay for a managed control plane and still own the node and add-on layer yourself. For three services, that overhead routinely outweighs the time spent on the actual product.

**A managed platform gives you most of what you'd want with a fraction of the surface.** You hand over a container image and get deploys, health checks, rolling updates, autoscaling, load balancing, logs, and secrets management out of the box. Cloud Run and Fargate scale to zero, so idle services cost next to nothing. Pair them with managed data services (RDS, Cloud SQL, managed Redis or queues) and your own services stay stateless — which is exactly what keeps them cheap to run and easy to move.

## When the answer flips to Kubernetes

Choose K8s only if at least one of these is true *now*, not "someday":

- **You already run a mature K8s platform** with a team operating it. Matching the house standard beats being the exception.
- **You have a hard requirement managed platforms can't meet:** a specific service mesh, GPU scheduling, a self-hosted stateful system with particular storage semantics, or strict on-prem/regulatory constraints. Even here, the first move is usually to offload state to managed data services and put only the genuinely special workload on K8s — not your whole app tier.
- **You're confidently heading to many services** and want one substrate now. Fair — but call it what it is: a bet. And migrating *to* K8s later from plain containers is routine, not a rewrite.

## Concrete recommendation

- **On GCP:** Cloud Run.
- **On AWS:** ECS/Fargate.
- **Cloud-agnostic, want the simplest developer experience:** Fly.io or Render.

Keep each service in a plain Dockerfile. If you ever outgrow the platform, moving to Kubernetes becomes a deployment-config change rather than a rewrite — so adopting it early buys you nothing you couldn't buy later, on better information.

The one thing that would sharpen this: if one of the three services self-hosts a stateful system you can't move to a managed service, or has unusual networking needs, tell me what the services actually do and I'll tighten the pick. Absent that, simpler hosting is the call — the best architecture decision here is the one that stays easy to reverse, and that's this one.
